coredata.dc:title: Pyridinic-N-rich carbon dots in IFE-based Turn-off Fluorometric detection of nerve agent Mimic– Diethyl chlorophosphate and multicolor cell imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Mondal, Imran
Samanta, Dipanjan
Shaw, Manisha
Abdus Salam Shaik, Md
Kr Mahto, Madhusudan
Basu, Rajarshi
Bhattacharya, Angana
Pathak, Amita
coredata.dc:description: Fluorometric sensors for the detection of nerve agent mimics have received a lot of interest nowadays due to their high sensitivity and selectivity, ease of operation, and real-time monitoring. Pyridinic-N-rich carbon dots (NCDs) prepared through microwave-assisted pyrolysis of l-Malic acid and urea have been explored first time in this work as a novel turn-off fluorescent probe for the sensitive and selective detection of diethyl chlorophosphate (DCP), a nerve agent mimic. The as-prepared carbon dots contained a large amount of pyridinic nitrogen on their surface, which can modulate the photoluminescence properties of the NCDs. The blue emissive NCDs possessed both excitation wavelength-dependent and independent emission behavior. The detection of DCP was premised on quenching of the fluorescence emission intensity of NCDs in the presence of similar chemical reagents (e.g., trimethyl phosphate, triethyl phosphate, triethyl phosphonoacetate, triphenyl phosphate, diphenyl phosphate, tributyl phosphate). Fluorescence quenching of the NCDs in the presence of DCP has been attributed to the inner filter effect (IFE). From the linear Stern-Volmer plot (R2 = 0.9992), the limit of detection (LOD) was found to be 84 μM for sensing DCP for the concentration ranging between 3 and 15 mM. The biocompatibility of NCDs was assessed through cytotoxicity assay on MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cells. Fluorescence imaging demonstrated that NCDs have low cytotoxicity and can be employed successfully in cell imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pyridinic- N-rich Carbon Dots
Nerve agent
DCP
Fluorescence
IFE
Multicolor cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of nerve agent mimic, DCP
Sensing of nerve agent mimic using NCDs as a fluorescent Probe
Application of DCP detection in real water samples
Cellular uptake and cytotoxicity assay of NCDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to acknowledge Prof. M. Mandal for providing the opportunity to carry out the cellular imaging study. I.M. would also like to acknowledge IIT Kharagpur, India for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) XRD pattern of the NCDs (B) TEM image and HRTEM image (inset) of the NCDs. (C) Histogram of the NCDs' size distribution. (D) Raman spectrum of the NCDs.
(A) XPS full survey of the NCDs. (B − D) Deconvolated spectra of C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s of the NCDs.
Zeta Potential (mV) variation of NCDs at different pH levels.
(A) UV–visible absorption, excitation and fluorescence emission spectra of the NCDs. (B) The normalized fluorescence emission spectra of the NCDs at various excitation wavelengths.

(A) Emission spectra of NCDs measured after successive addition of DCP (0–30 mM) gradually at 25 °C at λex = 340 nm (Inset: images of the NCDs solution under UV light without DCP on the left and with DCP (15 mM) on the right).(B) The Stern-Volmer plot for the fluorescence quenching of NCDs on the addition of DCP (Inset: dependence of F0/F value on the concentration of DCP within the range 0–30 mM).

(A) Comparison of the following the addition of different organophosphates at the same concentration. (B) Relative fluorescence intensity of NCDs (at λem = 445 nm) toward DCP and interference of other organophosphates with DCP.

(A) Stern‑Volmer plots for the NCD-DCP system at different temperatures (Inset: plot of F0/F with the concentration of DCP at different temperatures). (B) Fluorescence decays of the NCDs in the absence and presence of different concentrations of DCP. (C) Spectral overlap between excitation and fluorescence emission spectra of NCDs with UV–vis absorption spectrum of DCP. (D) UV–vis absorption spectra of NCDs in the presence of different concentrations of DCP.
The suppressed efficiency of observed and corrected fluorescence intensity for the NCDs after addition of different concentrations of DCP.

(a) Cellular uptake images of MDA-MB-231 Cells subjected to NCDs at excitation wavelengths of 405 (blue), 488 (green), and 561 (red) nm. Arrows show the cellular accumulation of NCDs (Scale bars: 20 µm). (b) Cytotoxicity of NCDs on MDA-MB-231 cells. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
The element analysis results from XPS survey spectra.
Comparison of various fluorescent probes for the detection of DCP.

row.entry: Organotin compounds of α-amino acid Schiff bases
Anthracene-bisimidazole tetraacid linker-based metal − organic nanosheets
6,7-dihydroxycoumarin embedded in an polyacrylamide hydrogel matrix

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Results from Stern‑Volmer plots for the NCD-DCP system at different temperatures.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Kq, quenching rate constant of DCP.

head.ce:title: Pyridinic-N-rich carbon dots in IFE-based Turn-off Fluorometric detection of nerve agent Mimic– Diethyl chlorophosphate and multicolor cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, W.B. 721302, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology Kharagpur, W.B. 721302, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorometric sensors for the detection of nerve agent mimics have received a lot of interest nowadays due to their high sensitivity and selectivity, ease of operation, and real-time monitoring. Pyridinic-N-rich carbon dots (NCDs) prepared through microwave-assisted pyrolysis of l-Malic acid and urea have been explored first time in this work as a novel turn-off fluorescent probe for the sensitive and selective detection of diethyl chlorophosphate (DCP), a nerve agent mimic. The as-prepared carbon dots contained a large amount of pyridinic nitrogen on their surface, which can modulate the photoluminescence properties of the NCDs. The blue emissive NCDs possessed both excitation wavelength-dependent and independent emission behavior. The detection of DCP was premised on quenching of the fluorescence emission intensity of NCDs in the presence of similar chemical reagents (e.g., trimethyl phosphate, triethyl phosphate, triethyl phosphonoacetate, triphenyl phosphate, diphenyl phosphate, tributyl phosphate). Fluorescence quenching of the NCDs in the presence of DCP has been attributed to the inner filter effect (IFE). From the linear Stern-Volmer plot (R2 = 0.9992), the limit of detection (LOD) was found to be 84 μM for sensing DCP for the concentration ranging between 3 and 15 mM. The biocompatibility of NCDs was assessed through cytotoxicity assay on MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cells. Fluorescence imaging demonstrated that NCDs have low cytotoxicity and can be employed successfully in cell imaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Human health is significantly affected by wars, endangering civilian survival. Some of the deadliest weapons that can cause horrific devastation are chemical warfare agents (CWAs). Phosphoric acid esters – Tabun (GA, C5H11N2O2P), Sarin (GB, C4H10FO2P), and Soman (GD, C7H16FO2P), are structurally related to a larger family of organophosphorus compounds (OPs), collectively known as the G-series nerve agents [1] and comprise a large portion of the chemical warfare agents. These compounds are highly toxic and lethal in both liquid and vapor forms and they tend to disrupt the message transfer mechanism of the nerves to various organs [2]. Upon inhalation or dermal penetration, they disrupt the nervous system by interfering with the degradation of the neurotransmitter- acetylcholine, via inhibition of the acetylcholinesterase enzyme, which causes continuous overstimulation of muscles that can eventually lead to death or permanent damage to the nervous system even at nonlethal doses [3]. The high toxicity and ease of synthesis of these agents from readily available industrial resources make them the choicest munitions in chemical warfare and thus a source of the ongoing threat to public health and the environment. Their menace persists even when they are not in use but are being disposed of from stockpile through incineration.

Global concern about the effect of chemical warfare agents on the biotic and abiotic segments of our ecosystem makes them a matter of significant interest in chemical, biochemical, and toxicological research where the development of easy and affordable methods for their rapid, selective, and sensitive detection holds great relevance. Several different approaches have been reported for the detection of these nerve agents over the past few decades, which include the potentiometric method, [4,5] colorimetric method, [6] surface acoustic wave spectroscopy, [7] photoacoustic spectroscopy, [8] molecular imprinting coupled with lanthanide luminescence and fibre optics, [9] enzymatic assay, [10] gas chromatography/mass spectrometry, [11] and interferometry [12]. Among the several detection strategies, the most convenient and easy means of chemical detection, which have attracted significant attention, is the use of chromogenic and fluorogenic sensors in which an optical event is utilized as a readout, such as a change in absorption or fluorescence intensity/wavelength in presence of the target analyte [13]. In recent years, fluorogenic sensors in particular have attracted great interest and garnered wide popularity as a detection tool in realistic sensing schemes due to their high sensitivity, specificity, and portability. Moreover, they have the ability to detect analytes in real time through an easy and cost-effective approach using tailor-made fluorescent molecules/compounds as sensing probes [14].

Different kinds of fluorophores have been successfully used for the detection of chemical warfare agent mimics which include- polynorbornene derived 8-hydroxyquinoline (NCHQ) and its polymer, [15] polymeric aggregates with a polysiloxanic hydrophobic core, [16] borondipyrromethene (BODIPY)-containing structures, [17] coumarin-based structure with double reactive groups, [18] conjugated polymer of poly(fluorene-co-quinoxaline), [19] 2,6-bis(1′-methylbenzimidazolyl)pyridine (Mebip) complexes, [20] organotin compounds of α-amino acid Schiff bases, [21] napthothiazolium conjugated benzothiazole derivative (NTBT), [22] carbazole–pyrrole conjugate (CPC), [23] N,N-carbonyl-bridged dipyrrinone oxime, [24] Anthracene-bisimidazole tetraacid linker-based metal − organic nanosheets, [25] 4-(6-(tertbutyl) pyridine-2-yl)-N,N-diphenylaniline, (TBPY-TPA), [26] 5′-(dimethylamino)-2′-formyl-N-hydroxy- [1,1′-biphenyl]-2-carboxamide(PTS) [27]. All the previously reported fluorogenic schemes for the detection of CWA mimics are limited by tedious synthetic procedures, time-consuming and expensive. Therefore, developing a simple, reliable, eco-friendly, cost-effective, time-efficient detection scheme for the detection of nerve agents still remains a major challenge. In this regard, the current work puts forward the use of Carbon Dots, as a fluorescent probe for the detection of G-series nerve agent mimics for the first time.

Carbon Dots (CDs) have received significant attention in the last decade due to their unique excitation-dependent photoluminescence property, resistance to photobleaching, biocompatibility, aqueous solubility, low intrinsic toxicity, and chemical versatility [28,29]. These fascinating physical and chemical properties make CDs favorable as a fluorescent sensor/ probe for the detection of analytes in various in vitro as well as in vivo applications. One of the noteworthy challenges involved in the use of fluorescent CDs is their low quantum yield. It has been recognized that the quantum yield and the multicolor photoluminescence (PL) property of the CDs can be significantly enhanced by incorporating suitable electron-rich heteroatoms through charge transfer between the electron-rich species and the electron-deficient carbon atoms [30]. Nitrogen-containing functional groups incorporated onto the CDs surfaces during their synthesis, from nitrogen-rich carbogenic precursors, tend to passivate their surface which eventually contributes to their enhanced fluorescence intensity.

In this work, pyridinic-N-rich carbon dots (NCDs) have been reported and explored for their application as a turn-off fluorescent probe for the detection of nerve agent mimic (or stimulant) based on the inner filter effect (IFE). The rationale behind the use of mimics is the non-availability of nerve agents (i.e., Sarin, Soman, and Tabun) in the open market. Diethyl chlorophosphate (DCP), diethyl fluorophosphate (DFP), and diethyl cyanophosphonate (DCNP) possess chemical structures and activity similar to that of the nerve agents but are far less toxic and commonly used as mimic molecules in all analytical studies involving nerve agents [31–34]. The present sensing studies are however restricted only to DCP. Furthermore, NCDs with good biocompatibility were successfully utilized in multicolor cell imaging.

NCDs were prepared through the microwave-assisted pyrolysis technique. Briefly, 10 mL of an aqueous solution containing 0.37 M l-Malic acid and 0.74 M urea was subjected to a 270 W microwave irradiation for 3 min and 30 s. During the heating process, the mixture turned from colorless liquid to a brown-colored solid, indicating NCDs formation. The brown solid product was filtered through a 0.22 μm PTFE membrane after being diluted with milli-Q water, and dialyzed for 24 h using a dialysis membrane against milli-Q water and positioned for freeze-drying. Finally, the freeze-dried NCDs were kept at 4 °C for future usage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of nerve agent mimic, DCP
ce:section.ce:para: The freeze-dried NCDs powder was dissolved in 50:50 (v/v) water/ethanol solutions for the preparation of a 1 mg/mL stock solution. 1.0 M stock solutions of various organophosphate such as trimethyl phosphate (TMP), triethyl phosphate (TEP), tributyl phosphate (TBP), triethyl phosphonoacetate (TEPA), diphenyl phosphate (DPP), triphenyl phosphate (TPP), diethyl cyanophosphonate (DCNP), and diethyl chlorophosphate (DCP) were prepared in 50:50 (v/v) water/ethanol solutions. To estimate the detection range, various sets of mixtures of fixed volumes were made by mixing varying quantities of DCP into a fixed volume of NCDs. Each solution was incubated for 5 min at room temperature followed by prompt fluorescence measurement. In order to assess the selectivity and anti-interference ability of NCDs, several organophosphate solutions in a fixed volume were separately mixed into a fixed volume of NCDs solutions in the presence and absence of DCP followed by incubation for 5 min at room temperature. Afterwards, the fluorescence emission spectrum of each set of solutions was recorded thrice at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm, and the emission intensity was measured at a wavelength of 445 nm.

MTT assay was performed with MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cells to assess the cytotoxicity of the NCDs. Approximately 5 × 105 cells/well were cultured in a plate, containing 96 flat-bottomed wells for 24 h at 37 °C which was then exposed to a series of concentrations of NCDs in the range of 0 to 300 μg/mL for 48 h at 37 °C. After incubation for 48 h, the supernatant, having suspended NCDs were removed from each well. Then 80 μL MTT [3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide] (1 mg /mL in PBS) solution was introduced into each well and each was then incubated further for 4 h at 37 °C. Following incubation, the formazan crystals in each well were dissolved in 100 μL DMSO and their absorbance was then recorded by using a microplate reader at 570 nm. The cell viability has been determined by following standard procedure [35]. For greater precision, the assay was performed three times.

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of NCDs (Fig. 1 A) displayed a broad diffraction peak centered at 26.2° with an interlayer spacing of 3.39 Å, which could be assigned to (002) diffraction plane of the graphite (d002 = 3.34 Å) [36]. A marginally larger value of the interlayer spacing in NCDs compared to graphitic carbon was possibly due to the incorporation of nitrogen and oxygen in the system. TEM studies of the as-synthesized NCDs, shown in Fig. 1B & 1C, revealed uniformly distributed particles (in the range of 2.5–6 nm) of spherical morphology with average diameters of ∼ 4 nm. The HRTEM image of the NCDs, presented as an inset in Fig. 1B, showed well-resolved lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.34 nm which could be ascribed to the (002) plane of the graphitic carbon, in conformity with XRD observations, and hence affirming the graphitic nature of the sample.

Raman spectra of NCDs, as shown in Fig. 1D, exhibited a peak at 1592 cm−1 which could be assigned to the G band corresponding to the E2g mode of vibration of sp2-bonded carbon atoms in graphite, and another peak at 1354 cm−1, corresponding to the A1g mode of vibrations of sp3 hybridized carbon atoms in the terminal plane of graphite. The G band of graphite in the Raman spectrum signifies the fraction of graphitized carbon in the sample while the D band represents the contributions from defects, edge effect, and functionalization [37]. The higher intensity of the G band over the D band emphasized the graphitic nature of NCDs. The shift in the G band of the NCDs compared to the pure graphitic G band could be attributed to the presence of defective graphitic structure in the NCDs.

Analysis of the FTIR spectra of NCDs (Fig. 2 ) revealed a broad peak at 3100–3450 cm−1 which could be due to the presence of hydroxyl and amine functional groups. The small band at 2766 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching frequency of CH bonds. The peaks at 1702 cm−1 and 1660 cm−1 were assigned to the amide I vibration and CC/CN stretching vibration respectively while the peaks at 1395 and 1345 cm−1 could be ascribed to CN stretching vibration [38,39]. The peaks at 1185 cm−1,1100 cm−1 and 763 cm−1 can be attributed to vibrational modes of CO stretching, COC stretching and CH out-of-plane bending respectively [40,41]. The sharp peak at 637 cm−1 may be associated with the out-of-plane bending of NH bonds [41]. FTIR studies thus indicate the formation of the pyridine moiety and also unveiled the presence of − OH, –CO, −COOH, –CONR, and –NH2 functional groups on the surface of NCDs [39].

The XPS spectrum of NCDs depicted in Fig. 3 A shows the typical C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s signals having binding energy 284, 399, and 531 eV respectively. This revealed the presence of C (57.9 %), O (26.3 %), and N (15.8 %) in the carbon dots. In the deconvoluted C 1 s spectrum, as displayed in Fig. 3B, the binding energy peaks at 283.9 eV, 285.2 eV, and 287.3 eV could be assigned to CC/CC, CN, and CO/CN respectively [42–44]. The binding energy peak at 398.5 eV in the deconvoluted N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3C) could be attributed to pyridinic-N, and another peak at 399.6 could be assigned to amidic/amino-N which was possibly get generated on pyrolysis of urea during the preparation [45,46]. Further, the peak at 530.5 and 531.6 eV from the deconvolution of the O 1 s peak (Fig. 3D) could be denoted to CO and, COC/C–OH respectively [47]. The element analysis results from XPS survey spectra were summarized in Table 1 . The XPS studies thus ascertain that the N atoms in the NCDs were present predominantly in the form of pyridine-like N. The outcomes were well consistent with the corresponding FT-IR data and confirmed the formation of NCDs.

The surface charge on the NCDs' surface was determined using the zeta potential measurement. The surface charge potentials of NCDs at different pH values were displayed in Fig. 4 . The surface charge potential of the basic (pH = 10) solutions of NCDs was found to be –32.2 mV whereas the neutral (pH = 7) and acidic (pH = 2) solutions of NCDs exhibited surface charges of −18.7 mV and + 5.27 respectively. The isoelectric point (IEP) of the NCDs was found to be 3.95, indicating that the surface of NCDs was surrounded by ample negatively charged carboxyl (–COOH) and hydroxyl (–OH) functional groups that contribute to long-term colloidal stability. It can be ascertained that in acidic conditions, all of the amine groups attached to the surface of NCDs remained protonated, whereas all of the amine and carboxyl groups present on the NCDs' surface remained deprotonated in basic conditions. Zeta potential analysis was in close agreement with FTIR and XPS results, which support the formation of numerous functional groups including carboxyl, carbonyl, hydroxyl, and amino groups surrounding the NCDs and the successful incorporation of N functionalities into the sp2-conjugated structure of the NCDs.

The optical properties of the NCDs were investigated by UV − vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. The UV − vis absorption spectrum of NCDs (Fig. 5 A) revealed two broad and weak absorption bands in the region 280–310 nm and 330–380 nm respectively. The band in the 280–310 nm region can be ascribed to the π → π* transition of the CC bonds, probably originating from the carbon core whereas the band in the 330–380 nm region may be attributed to the n → π* transition of heteroatomic functionalities such as CO/CN groups on the surface of the NCDs [48]. The excitation and fluorescence emission spectra of the NCDs were delineated in Fig. 5A and a noticeable color change of the NCDs solution was observed after exciting at 365 nm with a UV lamp (Figure S4). Following exposure to 340 nm irradiation, the NCDs solution exhibits a strong blue emission at 445 nm (Figure S1). The surface of the NCDs had a substantial impact on the optical properties of the NCDs because it controls the trapping of excitons during excitation. Further, the radiative recombination of the excitons trapped on the surface leads to fluorescence emission [49]. The fluorescence emission spectra of NCDs at different excitation wavelengths (λex) as displayed in Fig. 5B, demonstrates that when the excitation wavelength (λex) is changed from 320 to 360 nm, the emission peak (λem) remains almost unchanged at 445 nm. The emission peak (λem) however undergoes a red-shifted from 455 to 535 nm when the excitation wavelength (λex) is changed from 380 to 460 nm, which revealed the existence of both excitation-dependent and excitation-independent phenomena according to the excitation wavelength range. This excitation-independent emission behavior of NCDs in the blue region (at λex = 320–360 nm) was either due to π → π* transitions in the graphitic structure of the carbon core or the presence of pyridinic moiety as well as hydroxyl groups on the NCDs' surface as evident from the XPS studies. It has been reported that the excitation-dependent emission in the visible region (λex = 380–460 nm) may arise due to the distribution of different emissive sites/centers on each NCDs surface, which could be attributed to the presence of different types of functional groups (CO/CN) on the surface of NCDs, having different energy levels resulted in a number of emissive traps that govern the emission at different excitation wavelengths [49].

This bathochromic shift in the fluorescence emission (λem) with the increase in the excitation wavelengths (λex) makes NCDs unique for various applications as λem could be tailored by altering the λex. The presence of N atoms in the NCDs seems to play an important role in tuning the optical properties of the NCDs. Using quinine sulfate as a reference, the quantum yield of synthesized NCDs is calculated to be 33 %. In this work, the high quantum yield could be due to the incorporation of electronegative N atoms and the existence of hydroxyl and amide groups on the surface of NCDs. The incorporation of N atoms can substantially change the whole electronic structure in NCDs and increased the spin-orbital coupling for the intersystem crossing emission process, whereas the presence of surface functional groups might significantly increase the intrinsic state emission while efficiently suppressing the non-radiative recombination of localized electron-hole pairs resulting in the photoluminescence and a substantial increase in the quantum yield of NCDs [50].

The effect of pH on the fluorescence emission intensity of the NCDs was also studied at different pH values. As depicted in Fig. S2A the emission intensity of the NCDs solution gradually decreased in the acidic range of pH. However, the emission intensity of NCDs decreases significantly in a strongly alkaline environment (pH 12). The drop in emission intensity in highly basic conditions could be ascribed to the deprotonation of carboxyl acid groups and dissociation of amide bonds whereas in a highly acidic solution this observation could be explained on the basis of the aggregation behavior of NCDs that leads to the non-radiative recombination. The change in fluorescence intensity of NCDs in different solvents was shown in Fig. S2B. Fluorescence intensity was observed to be higher in the case of polar protic solvent, which might be due to enhanced solvation that suppresses non-radiative decay. The effects of UV-light illumination and ionic strength on the fluorescence emission of NCDs were depicted in Figure S3, which showed that the NCDs were highly stable under a high-salt environment and UV-light illumination for 1 h. The high photostability of NCDs could make them a suitable candidate for fluorescent probes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of nerve agent mimic using NCDs as a fluorescent Probe
ce:section.ce:para:
The as-prepared NCDs was used as a fluorescent turn-off probe for the sensitive detection of highly toxic G-type nerve agent mimic– diethyl chlorophosphate (DCP) which have been commonly used as model compounds for the design of markers and sensors because of their comparable chemical structures and reactivity but do not have the efficacy of conventional nerve agents (i.e., sarin, soman and tabun) [33,34]. The NCDs exhibited bright blue fluorescence, excellent aqueous solubility, high photostability and excitation wavelength-dependent emission behavior. It was noticed that DCP could quench the fluorescence emission of NCDs significantly. The fluorescence emission spectra of solutions of NCDs before and after the successive addition of DCP at 340 nm excitation wavelength were displayed in Fig. 6 A. The maximum emission intensity of NCDs (i.e., λem = 445 nm) was found to gradually decrease with the successive addition of DCP (0–30 mM). The quenching efficiency of NCDs (i.e., F 0 /F) can be finely fitted to the Stern-Volmer equation:
(1)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K
SV
[ Q ]
= 1 +
k q
τ 0
[ Q ]

where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of NCDs in the absence and presence of DCP at λem = 445 nm, kq represents the quenching rate constant, τ0 is the fluorescence average lifetime of the NCDs, KSV is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant, and [Q] is the quencher concentration i.e., DCP (0–30 mM). The fitted plot, depicted in Fig. 6B, exhibited a good linear correlation between F0/F and the concentrations of DCP with a correlation coefficient (R2) value of 0.9992. The limit of detection (LOD) value of DCP, calculated using
3 σ
criteria, [42] where σ is the standard deviation of the measurement, was found to be 84 μM in the linear concentration range of 3–15 mM. The quenching studies were repeated 5 times to get a LOD value with better accuracy. NCDs were compared with previously reported fluorescent probes for the real-time detection of DCP and the findings are listed in Table 2 .

The selectivity studies in the detection of DCP have been carried out by measuring the fluorescence response under NCDs in presence of a series of organophosphorus compounds having structural similarity with DCP such as trimethyl phosphate, triethyl phosphate, triethyl phosphonoacetate, triphenyl phosphate, diphenyl phosphate, tributyl phosphate, and diethyl cyanophosphonate. The change in the relative intensities of NCDs (F/F0) has been measured and depicted through a histogram in Fig. 7 A, where, F and F0 are the fluorescence intensities in the presence and absence of DCP and each of the different organophosphorus compounds respectively. From the histogram, it can be observed that the relative intensities of the various organophosphorus compounds showed no considerable change compared to DCP. It is noteworthy that NCDs showed some extent of quenching in the fluorescence intensity in the presence of DCNP and TPP as shown in Figure S5, but the value of KSV for DCNP (
0.13 ×
10
2
) and TPP (
0.08 ×
10
2
) is significantly low as compared to that for DCP. Further, the anti-interference ability of the NCDs towards various interfering compounds was studied to explore the effect of interferences on the relative fluorescence intensity of NCDs in the presence of DCP. As shown in Fig. 7B, interfering compounds of the same concentration as DCP made no pronounced interference (changes of relative intensities were<10 %). This validates the selectivity of NCDs towards DCP over other organophosphorus compounds.

Fluorescence quenching may be explained by several mechanisms, including Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), ground-state complex formation and the inner filter effect (IFE). Although linearity in the Stern–Volmer plot is not adequate to ascertain the fluorescence quenching, it is still a well-accepted method to discriminate between the quenching mechanisms. The linear Stern–Volmer plot (Fig. 6B) reveals that the quenching mechanism is either dynamic (collisional) or static. In the dynamic quenching process, fluorescence quenching has been credited to the diffusion-controlled collision between the excited fluorophore and the quencher. In contrast, the quenching of fluorescence emission intensity in the static quenching process is known to be caused by the interaction of the quencher and the fluorophore in the ground state, which results in the formation of a less fluorescent or non-fluorescent ground state complex. To ascertain the fluorescence quenching mechanism of NCDs in the presence of DCP, quenching experiments were carried out at three different temperatures, viz. 283, 298 and 313 K. Stern-Volmer plot for the three different temperatures was depicted in Fig. 8 A and the respective KSV and kq values, calculated from the Stern-Volmer equation (i.e., Equation (1)), have been tabulated in Table 3 . The Stern–Volmer constant (KSV) values which were the measure of the interactions between NCDs and DCP were found to decrease with an increase in the temperature, indicating the possibility of a static quenching process.

To discriminate between the two mechanisms, the fluorescence decay kinetics of NCDs has been studied in the presence of different concentrations of DCP through Time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) measurements. In essence, the lifetime represents the rate of recombination of electron-hole pairs and is determined by their wave function overlapping [54]. As depicted in Fig. 8B, the fluorescence decay of NCDs could be nicely fitted by a tri-exponential function and the corresponding TCSPC data was summarized in Table S1, which revealed three decay components. The fast one could be attributed to the nonradiative recombination with a lifetime of 1.016 ns and the slow component should correspond to the radiative recombination (11.818 ns). With increasing DCP concentrations, the short lifetime decreases gradually from 1.016 to 0.803 ns, and the proportion of the fast component remained almost unchanged. So the process of electron transfer could be ruled out. For the NCDs, both the excitation and fluorescence spectra overlapped with the absorption spectrum of DCP (Fig. 8C), which is the utmost criterion for FRET or IFE to operate. In comparison, the overlapping of excitation/emission spectra of NCDs with the absorption spectrum of other organophosphate compounds was shown in Figure S6. The average fluorescence lifetimes of NCDs were observed to remain almost unchanged with an increase in the concentration of DCP, invalidating the possibility of FRET between NCD and DCP. However, as depicted in Fig. 8D the absorption spectra of NCDs did not alter as a result of the addition of different concentrations of DCP and the FTIR spectra of the NCDs-DCP system, as shown in Figure S7, indicating no such new bond formation, which ruled out the possibility of ground-state complex formation between the NCD and DCP. Therefore, IFE could be a significant fluorescence quenching process in NCD. In contrast to other fluorescence quenching methods, IFE-based approaches offer excellent sensitivity, significant flexibility, and simplicity without the necessity of covalent linkage between the quencher and fluorophore [55]. According to the traditional IFE mechanism, nonradiative energy conversion results in a decrease in the fluorophore emission intensity when the fluorophore's (NCD) emission spectrum overlaps with the absorber's (DCP) absorption spectrum [56]. On the other hand, NCD's excitation and the DCP's absorption spectra overlapped to induce radiation shielding, which results in incident radiation competition between the fluorophore and absorber, leading to a significant reduction in fluorescence emission intensity.

To corroborate the occurrence of the IFE, the recorded emission intensity was corrected by using the Parker equation:
(2)
F
cor
F
obs
=
2.3 d
A
ex
1 -
10
- d
A
ex
10
g
A
em
2.3 s
A
em
1 -
10
- s
A
em

Where, Fobs is the recorded maximum fluorescence intensity and Fcor is the corrected maximum fluorescence intensity that is obtained by removing IFE from Fobs. Aex and Aem are the absorbance at the excitation wavelength (340 nm) and the maximum emission wavelength (445 nm), respectively. s is the thickness of the excitation beam (s = 0.10 cm). g is the distance between the edge of the excitation beam (g = 0.40 cm) and the edge of the cuvette and d is the width of the cuvette (d = 1.0 cm). Correction factor (CF = Fcor/Fobs), the recorded (Eobs) and corrected (Ecor) fluorescence quenching efficiencies were calculated using Equation (2) and listed in Table S2. It was found that about 60 % of the suppressed fluorescence quenching primarily was from the IFE (Fig. 9 ), which suggests that there was a major contribution of IFE to the overall quenching process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of DCP detection in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The developed fluorescent sensing probe was analysed for DCP levels in real water samples in order to test its applicability. For this analysis, we took tap water and pond water from the local area (Kharagpur, India). Different volume of DCP was added to 3 mL of each real water samples to make specific concentrations. Then fluorescence quenching experiment was performed on each sample to determine the concentration of added DCP and the findings were summarized in Table S3. For all the samples the recoveries of DCP in the real water samples were calculated to be greater than 90 %. The satisfactory outcomes showed the successful application of the fluorescent sensing probe for the detection of DCP in real water samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular uptake and cytotoxicity assay of NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The cellular uptake efficiency of NCDs by MDA-MB-231 cells at different time intervals was envisioned using fluorescence microscopy. The microscopic images of NCDs-conjugated MDA-MB-231 cells were obtained at different excitation wavelengths (405, 488, and 561 nm). From the Fig. 10 a, it can be inferred that the entrapment of carbon dots into the MDA-MB-231 cell line increases with the deposition of NCDs around the nucleus. After 15 min, the cells exhibited feeble blue, prominent green and red fluorescence. It was observed that fluorescence intensity in the cytoplasm and nuclear periphery increases with the time of laser exposure. Therefore, multicolor (feeble blue, green and red) fluorescence was observed in cells treated with NCDs when imaged on a fluorescence microscope at excitation wavelengths of 405 (blue), 488 (green), and 561 nm (red), respectively [57]. This result is in good agreement with fluorescence spectrometric measurements. Moreover, the carbon dots accumulation inside the cells with extended incubations enhanced the fluorescence intensity (data not shown). Therefore, cellular uptake studies suggest the cellular uptake efficiency of NCDs. The biocompatibility of NCDs was evaluated through cytotoxicity assay on the MDA-MB-231 cell line [Fig. 10b]. Cells were subjected to a range of concentrations of NCDs (0–280 µg/mL) and it was observed that the cell viability decreased approximately to 50 % at 280 µg/mL concentration of NCDs. This result suggests low toxicity and good biocompatible nature of the NCDs.

We have developed, for the first time, NCDs as a fluorescent turn-off probe for the real-time detection of DCP. A rapid and simple one-pot microwave-assisted pyrolysis method had been used to synthesize highly fluorescent pyridinic-N-rich Carbon Dots (NCDs) using urea as the source of nitrogen, and l-Malic acid as the source of carbon. The as-prepared NCDs were spherical and emitted bright blue fluorescence with a quantum yield of ∼ 33 %. NCDs were found to serve as an efficient fluorescent probe for the detection of G-series nerve agent mimics – diethyl chlorophosphate (DCP) in the concentration range of 3–15 mM with a limit of detection of 84 μM. Here, IFE was the major factor in the fluorescence quenching mechanism. Fluorescence imaging experiments revealed that NCDs are nontoxic to the cell and can be used for cell imaging with good sensitivity. Thus, it can be concluded that NCDs have enormous potential to be used for diagnostic purposes and as an effective probe for sensitively detecting real nerve agents.

Imran Mondal: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Validation. Dipanjan Samanta: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Manisha Shaw: Writing – review & editing. Md Abdus Salam Shaik: Writing – review & editing. Madhusudan Kr Mahto: Writing – review & editing. Rajarshi Basu: Writing – review & editing. Angana Bhattacharya: Writing – review & editing. Amita Pathak: .

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge Prof. M. Mandal for providing the opportunity to carry out the cellular imaging study. I.M. would also like to acknowledge IIT Kharagpur, India for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Materials, Characterization of NCDs, Quantum yield measurements, Excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence emission spectra of NCDs (Fig. S1), pH and solvent-dependent fluorescence emission spectra of NCDs (Fig. S2), Effects of UV-light illumination and ionic strength on the fluorescence emission of NCDs (Fig. S3), Images of NCDs under UV and visible light ( Fig. S4)), Fluorescence quenching study of NCDs in the presence of DCNP and TPP ( Fig. S5)), Overlap of the excitation/emission spectrum of NCDs with the absorption of OPs ( Fig. S6)), FTIR spectra of NCDs after addition of DCP ( Fig. S7)), Evaluation of TCSPC data (Table S1)), Fluorescence correction data (Table S2), Detection of DCP in real water samples (Table S3). Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.122530.

ce:section.ce:para:
